title: 7 2 Inverse Laplace transforms
body:
7.2 INVERSE LAPLACE TRANSFORMS

LAPLACE TRANSFORMS

Z ∞ L{f (t)} = e−st f (t)dt = F (s)

f (t) F (s) 1 1/s t 1/s2 tm m!/sm+1 tν , (ν > −1) Γ(ν + 1)/sν+1 e−at 1/(s + a) sin bt b/(s2 + b2 ) cos bt s/(s2 + b2 ) sinh bt b/(s2 − b2 ) cosh bt s/(s2 − b2 ) sin bt − bt cos bt 2b3 /(s2 + b2 )2 sin bt + bt cos bt 2bs2 /(s2 + b2 )2 t sin bt 2bs/(s2 + b2 )2 te−at 1/(s + a)2 u(t − c) e−cs /s e−at f (t) F (s + a) tf (t) −F 0 (s) f (t − c)u(t − c) e−cs F (s) f 0 (t) sF (s) − f (0) f 00 (t) s2 F (s) − sf (0) − f 0 (0) f 000 (t) s3 F (s) − s2 f (0) − sf 0 (0) − f 00 (0) Z t f (τ )dτ F (s)/s

In the previous lecture we used the Laplace transform to change f (t) to F (s) (written L(f ) = F ). We now want to undo this process and convert F (s) back to f (t). This is referred to as taking an inverse Laplace transform and we write f = L−1 (F ). There is no integral formula for taking inverse Laplace transforms and we have no option but to just use the Laplace transform table together with a bag of interesting tricks.

Example 1 Find the inverse Laplace transform of each of the following functions:

4 1 a) F (s) = + 2 ; s−2 s +9 3 12 2 b) F (s) = − 2 + 5. s s s

1 t4 F a) 4e2t + sin(3t) b) 3 − 12t + F 3 12

Observe that we are now using the table backwards and traveling from the s variable back to the t variable. In order to be able to invert a wider class of objects we need a uesful little step function called the Heaviside function.

Definition: The Heaviside function u(t) (also called the unit step function) is given by   0, t<0; u(t) = , t=0;  2 1, t>0. The graph of u(t) is:

Note that the behaviour of u(t) at t = 0 is somewhat cosmetic for our purposes and you may view the definition of the Heaviside function as being simply:  0, t<0; u(t) = 1, t≥0. with graph

Of greater importance to us is the function u(t − c).  0, t<c; u(t − c) = 1, t≥c. The graph of u(t − c) is then:

Always remember that the Heaviside function u(t − c) is a single mathematical “switch” that is off until c and then turns on. It is then equal to 1 to infinity. The Heaviside function can be viewed as the simplest possible discontinuous function.

Example 2 Sketch y = 9u(t − 4).

F

e−cs Example 3 Prove that L{u(t − c)} = . s

F

Note that the above result is part of your standard tables.

Example 4 Find the Laplace transform of 9u(t − 4).

F

We use the Heaviside function to build up other discontinuous functions. Since taking the Laplace transform is a process of integration we may be sloppy on the definition of a function at endpoints as it has no impact on the final result. When sketching however it is a good idea to put in the dot on the discontinuities.

 1, a ≤ t ≤ b; Example 5 Suppose that f (t) = 0, otherwise. Prove that f (t) = u(t − a) − u(t − b) .

So u(t − a) − u(t − b) is the “switch” function that is off until t = a, turns on until t = b, and then turns back off again.

Example 7 Sketch each of the following functions:

a) f (t) = 1 − u(t − 3)

b) f (t) = t2 u(t − 4) (This is a cut.)

c) f (t) = (t − 4)2 u(t − 4) (This is a shift plus cut.)

d) f (t) = t2 {u(t − 2) − u(t − 7)}

Example 8 Express the function f (t) = 3t + tu(t − 1) + u(t − 3) − 1 without the use of the Heaviside and sketch.
